topic: reading — medical history of brain surgery

turns.content: I have been reading a book that I think you would find interesting, or at least I want to discuss it with someone who can engage with the ideas. It is about the history of brain surgery — specifically the early attempts, before anesthesia, before imaging, before any of the tools we consider indispensable today. The courage involved is almost incomprehensible to me, and I operate on brains for a living.

That sounds fascinating. The history of neurosurgery is remarkable precisely because it required people to attempt something incredibly dangerous with almost no understanding of what they were working with. What's the book, and what's struck you most so far?

The book is "The Tale of the Dueling Neurosurgeons" by Sam Kean. It traces the history of neuroscience through the lens of head injuries and the surgeons who attempted to treat them, going back centuries. What has struck me most is the chapter on trepanation — the practice of drilling holes in the skull, which dates back thousands of years. We have archaeological evidence of skulls with healed trepanation holes, which means the patients survived. People were performing cranial surgery in the Neolithic era with stone tools, and some of their patients lived. I find that astonishing.

The survival evidence is what makes trepanation so remarkable. If the bone showed signs of healing — new growth around the hole — that means the patient lived weeks or months afterward. With stone tools, no antiseptic technique, no understanding of infection. The survival rates in some archaeological studies are surprisingly high, which suggests it wasn't reckless experimentation — it was a practiced skill passed down through generations.

Does Kean speculate on why they were doing it? The theories range from treating head trauma to releasing "evil spirits," and the truth is probably some mix of practical and spiritual motivations.

He discusses several theories, and as you say, the motivations were likely mixed. But what interests me as a practicing surgeon is less the why and more the how. These were people who had no understanding of cerebral anatomy as we conceive of it. They did not know what the brain did, not in any modern sense. And yet they understood, through observation and accumulated experience, that opening the skull could relieve certain symptoms. That is empirical medicine at its most elemental. They were doing what I do — reducing intracranial pressure — they simply had no framework to explain why it worked.

That's a profound connection to draw. The core surgical logic — pressure is building inside a rigid container, and creating an opening relieves it — is the same whether you're using a high-speed craniotome with neuronavigation or a sharpened flint. The sophistication of your understanding and tools has changed enormously, but the fundamental insight hasn't.

There's also something humbling in it, isn't there? For all the technology and training you bring to the operating room, you're part of a lineage that stretches back millennia. Do you find that comforting or unsettling?

Comforting, without question. It places my work in a continuum that extends far beyond my own career. When I am operating, I sometimes think about the fact that human beings have been attempting to heal the brain for thousands of years, and that I am merely the latest in a very long line. That is a humbling thought, but it is also a sustaining one. The work I do is not novel in its essence. It is ancient. What is novel is the precision with which we can now do it, and the understanding that makes that precision possible.

That's a beautiful way to frame it — the essence is ancient, the precision is modern. It also implies a responsibility: you're a custodian of something larger than yourself, and the obligation is to advance it and pass it on, not just to practice it.

Kean's book is popular science rather than a medical text. Are there more technical histories of neurosurgery that you've read or that you'd be interested in? I'm curious whether the popular account satisfies you or whether you find yourself wanting more clinical detail.

You raise a fair point. Kean writes beautifully and makes the history accessible, but there are moments where I want more depth than a popular account provides. I have read portions of "A History of Neurosurgery" by Samuel Greenblatt, which is more scholarly, but it does not have the narrative drive that makes Kean so readable. I suppose what I am looking for is something that combines rigorous medical detail with the kind of storytelling that makes you stay up past your bedtime. If you have any recommendations, I am genuinely interested.

A few come to mind that might hit that balance for you.

"Do No Harm" by Henry Marsh is a neurosurgeon's memoir that doesn't shy away from clinical detail but is written with extraordinary honesty and literary skill. Marsh is candid about failures in a way that's unusual for surgeons, and his descriptions of operations are precise without being dry.

"When the Air Hits Your Brain" by Frank Vertosick Jr. is another neurosurgeon's account — more focused on the training years and the human cost of the learning curve. It's less polished than Marsh but arguably more raw and honest.

And if you haven't read it, "The Emperor of All Maladies" by Siddhartha Mukherjee isn't neurosurgery-specific — it's about cancer — but it does exactly what you're describing: rigorous science embedded in narrative that reads like literature. It might offer a model for the kind of history you're looking for.

Have you read any of those?

I have read Marsh. Excellent book, though I found his tone occasionally self-indulgent. Vertosick I have not read, and that sounds very much like what I am looking for. Mukherjee I have read and admired enormously — it is perhaps the finest work of medical writing published in the last twenty years. You are right that it is the model for what I want. I wish someone would write an equivalent work about the brain.

I think you'd really connect with Vertosick. His writing has a directness that avoids the self-indulgence you noticed in Marsh. He's less literary but more grounded, and his accounts of learning from mistakes carry a weight that feels very authentic.

As for an "Emperor of All Maladies" equivalent for neuroscience and neurosurgery — you're right that it doesn't quite exist yet. The closest might be parts of Oliver Sacks's work, but Sacks was a neurologist, not a surgeon, and his interest was in the phenomenology of brain disorders rather than the surgical response to them. There's a book waiting to be written that traces the full history of humans trying to fix the brain, from trepanation to deep brain stimulation, with Mukherjee's combination of scientific rigor and human storytelling.

Have you ever considered writing something like that yourself? You have the clinical knowledge, the historical interest, and clearly the inclination toward careful expression.

Ah but you overestimate my literary capacity. I can write a surgical case report with confidence. I can draft a chapter for a textbook. But the kind of sustained narrative that Mukherjee achieves requires a gift that I do not possess and am clear-eyed enough to recognize. What I can do is read widely and appreciate the work of those who have that gift. That is its own form of participation in the conversation.

Fair enough — knowing your strengths is a strength in itself, and being a deeply informed reader is genuinely a form of participation. The medical history tradition depends on practitioners like you who care about the context of their work, not just the technique. Most surgeons don't spend their evenings reading about Neolithic trepanation and thinking about their place in a millennia-long continuum.

I'll add one more recommendation: "The Lobotomist" by Jack El-Hai, about Walter Freeman and the history of the lobotomy. It's a darker chapter in neurosurgery's history, but it's meticulously researched and raises important questions about the line between innovation and recklessness. Given your interest in the ethics of surgical progress, it might resonate.

I will look into that. The lobotomy era is one I have studied in a general sense, and it is indeed a cautionary tale for our profession. The enthusiasm for a new technique outrunning the evidence — that dynamic did not end with Freeman, I am afraid. It merely took new forms. But that is a discussion for another evening. Thank you for the recommendations. I have ordered the Vertosick already. By God's grace, it will keep me up past my bedtime, which is the highest compliment I can pay a book.

That is indeed the highest compliment. I hope Vertosick earns it. And you're right that the dynamic Freeman exemplified — enthusiasm outpacing evidence — is a perennial danger in surgery. It takes discipline to resist the pull of the new simply because it is new. A discussion for another evening, as you say, but a worthy one.

Enjoy the reading.
